Code fragment: public final TableField<LessonsRecord, UUID> ID = createField(DSL.name("id"), SQLDataType.UUID.nullable(false), this, "");

Code fragment: public final TableField<StudentsRecord, UUID> USER_ID = createField(DSL.name("user_id"), SQLDataType.UUID.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The 'user_id' column appears to reference a users table but lacks a foreign key constraint, which could lead to orphaned records or referential integrity issues. Add a foreign key constraint to ensure that every 'user_id' value corresponds to a valid user record.

Code fragment: public final TableField<TeachersRecord, UUID> ID = createField(DSL.name("id"), SQLDataType.UUID.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The primary key column is named just 'id', which is a generic name that doesn't convey meaning and can cause ambiguity in queries with joins. Consider renaming it to 'teacher_id' to make it more descriptive and self-documenting.

Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from all joined tables (LESSONS, STUDENTS, TEACHERS), which retrieves unnecessary data and impacts performance. Explicitly list only the required columns, e.g., `dsl.select(LESSONS.ID, LESSONS.STUDENT_ID, ...)`

The query() method uses select() without specifying columns, which fetches all columns from the STUDENTS table. Explicitly list only the required columns, e.g., .select(STUDENTS.ID, STUDENTS.USER_ID).

The query() method uses select() without specifying columns, which fetches all columns from the TEACHERS table. Explicitly list only the required columns, e.g., .select(TEACHERS.ID, TEACHERS.USER_ID).
